speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, Jerry Shane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Little-- very little is being mentioned about the-- after the war. The Brihah was an underground organization from Palestine, risking their lives, came to rescue Jewish people from Eastern Europe. I found myself amongst Jewish people by coincidence, by hitchhiking towards the United States. Instead I found myself in orphanages, orphanage after orphanage. I kept running away with my friend Chaim Boorstein from orphanage to orphanage. We found that this orphanage in this city gives you better food. So in one, in Cracow, for example, I was in two orphanages. One I was during the weekday, and then on weekends, I went to the other orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because why? The food was better on weekends the religious one, so I went over there. It wasn't an orphanage. It was a kibbutz, actually. How did it feel to be free?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't realize I was free. It was just part of my life, day in and day in and day in. Just an observer. That's all I felt like. I'm an observer. I didn't appreciate the freedom. I just appreciated that in fact, I am Jewish, and these-- everybody was Jewish around me. But I felt stranger being Jewish, because I didn't speak Yiddish no more. I was a stranger amongst my own people. So when--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel, though, to finally have someone else taking care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I didn't know how to take it. For example, my friend, Chaim Boorstein and I were hitchhiking towards the United States. We met up with Polish officers in the army. They were going to-- the one guy was going to adopt us. Instead, we wound up in jail. [INAUDIBLE] a Jewish man. And wound up in the orphanage in my hometown. The orphanage consisted of children who are being bought by the Brihah from Gentile people who harbored these children. During the war, the family-- the mother or father-- would give all the valuables to a Gentile family to harbor this child until after the war, when they were going to come and claim them. And after the war, they were going to give more valuables. Many parents did not survive and did not return. Many Jewish children of today living as Gentiles without knowing they are Gentiles. Lots of these children are now grown people of my age now, to discover their mother and their father-- adoptive mother and father, supposedly-- are dying. When they are dying, the priest come to give them the last rites. As they give them the last rites, they confess to the priest all their sins. This child over here-- mostly girls, it would be-- they-- she's not my daughter. She's a Jew. And that's why I mistreated her all the time and treated my children better. And now this woman, who has grandchildren who are married themselves also-- even the grandchildren are married-- the family realizes she's Jewish. Not only she's being-- she's antisemitic herself-- she's antisemite-- she's brought up to be antisemitic-- she discovered she's Jewish. She cannot go to the Jewish people, who are only less than 5,000. Elderly Jews don't want [NON-ENGLISH] in their midst. So they can then-- children, the grandchildren, do not want them. So there is a dilemma. And many of them, after the war ended, or even before the war ended-- children were sold to the Brihah. And this orphanage-- my orphanage was [INAUDIBLE] in my hometown, and many other orphanages surrounding, in different cities, centrally located. And many children were stolen back again by the Gentiles, and sell them back to another orphanage. The same child was sold and resold. There's a woman over here, Ida Kirsch, who found her step-- twin brother, in the Children of the Holocaust Organization where I belong. Her brother was sold and resold to this orphanage, which I was at the first time in Lublin. And then his aunt-- through adopted, the people who kept him-- gave birth to a little girl, and the mother died in childbirth of the little girl. They sold the little child as a Jewish child, and stole it again and again. It gets sold to many other orphanages. They made a deal with him to run away from the orphanage and come back to collect more money for him in different places. Many of these children were smuggled out-- I was amongst them-- from Poland at the proper, opportune time by the Brihah, paid the border guards when the-- with whiskey or whatever they would have wanted to let us across. I came across the border as a Greek Jew, not supposed to speak Yiddish or Polish, returning from concentration camp. My name was Yakov [INAUDIBLE]. And at this same way, they brought us to Czechoslovakia. In Czechoslovakia, we stayed in Prague, at a camp. From the camp they got us fictitious papers with pictures as we are German Jews returning to Germany, and smuggled us out of Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, we had to cross the border again into Austria, because Austria knew how to read German. At night, holding by a rope, so we would not get lost. The only strong ones could do. And we wound up in Vienna, and they put us on trucks in the middle of the night, and transported us to Landsberg, near-- near Landsberg, in [INAUDIBLE]. Landsberg is the city where Hitler wrote I was in a kibbutz at 400. I decided to, if they wanted to go to Israel or to Palestine, that was the only thing for a Jewish child like myself. There were 400 of us. And I see another-- one of the boys had a cousin. I was jealous of him. So I went to look-- to Munich to see if I could find family in Chicago of my stepfather's side. And I wrote to everybody in Chicago in Polish, asking them if they were related to me under the name of [INAUDIBLE]. My name would be Ciesinski. Nobody answered me except one man, Josef Ciesinski-- Joseph Ciesinski-- and he told me he was my uncle and sent me a picture, which I have on me right now. And in the picture, underlined Aunt Mary and two cousins-- girl cousins. And he started sending me packages. I ran away from the kibbutz to a displaced persons camp named Neu Ulm, a displaced person camp where Jewish people were returning from the Soviet Union-- Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you want to stay with the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to come to look for-- for my family. I wanted to have a family that belongs to me. And if I had stayed in the kibbutz and communicated with my family in-- in the United States, I would have been expelled in disgrace for a traitor for the cause. It was [INAUDIBLE] to go to the United States. And that's when I realized it was the proper thing for me to go and to stay in the Galud, in exile, as what happened to my parents. And just for curiosity, I wanted to see somebody that belongs to me. After having nobody-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it-- you see, when you are amongst other people who are the same as you are, you don't miss it. But someday you stop. You see-- one boy had a cousin. I was so jealous of him. Not envy-- jealous. I wanted to have somebody that belongs to me. And I wrote to everybody, except, when I wound up in a displaced persons camp, Neu Ulm, and in Neu Ulm I knew a Marcel, a musician. He also was there with his sister, and his family came from the Soviet Union and was a member of the organization that I belonged to too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started writing to them, and they started sending me packages. And there was another family from my hometown-- a young couple with two little babies-- and they told me, why do you write to your uncle in Polish? Let me write a Yiddish letter. You are going to get more packages. I listened to them, and they wrote my Yiddish letter for me. Everything came to a standstill. Nothing was coming forward, no packages, nothing, not even letters. I started bombarding my uncle with letters, why. He kept asking, what should I send to you? And I said toba-- cigarettes and coffee, which I could trade for other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I don't want coffee. I don't want no cigarettes, just tell him, I want you. I need family. And finally, a letter came from Mr. [? Nedboy, ?] in Yiddish, that said, I'm Mr. [? Nedboy, ?] and I have a shoe manufacturing in Chicago. And Joseph Ciesinski is not your uncle. I'm sorry to disappoint you at a time like this, under the circumstances. I feel very bad about it, but unfortunately, Mr. Ciesinski, who works for me, he's not even Jewish. He's Gentile. But I'm going to try to help her find your family. I found my stepfather's cousins, the family of my first cousins, supposedly. They made my life very unbearable, and it took me about 40-some odd years later, my late wife, Helen, convinced me or forced me to go to Israel and look my mother's sister up. And I did the same thing, and when I arrived in Jerusalem, called everybody in Haifa by the name of [? Kisman ?]. And I found my mother's sister's husband, her three children, and their children, and my uncle-- my mother's sister's husband, [INAUDIBLE]. I just spoke to him a couple days ago and I'm coming to visit him now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you finally get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to come to the United States. The Jewish Children's Bureau, the Care of European Children, felt they should not send me to the United States while I have family in the United States. They're going to send priority children who do not have family. As an orphan, I had priority.

speaker: interviewer
text: My quarter-- Polish quarter-- I could come right away to the United States in 1945. But they felt, why send me? Let my family sponsor me. My family didn't want me, because they were afraid I want to have to share the inheritance from my grandmother, my aunt. And I wasn't interested in any money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, I lingered on for three extra years in this-- in Germany, in a displaced persons prison camp, to knocking on every door, asking everybody to help me out. And finally the Jewish Children's Bureau brought me to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 21st, 1948, which is one of the dates I only remember out of all the dates I do not remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got here, I stayed with my cousins, who didn't want me. And they made my life miserable, because the cousin [INAUDIBLE] forced them to keep me and send me to school, as a-- because the money was left for me too, not just for them, from my family. And I didn't want their money. I just-- went away and I'll try to learn a vocation. Right now, presently, I have two beautiful daughters, wonderful sons-in-law, and four grandchildren-- two grandchildren, two children out of each daughter. Boy, girl, boy, and girl. And they married to a Weinstein and a Kaplan, to carry on the tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you feel you adjusted to life here in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult for me to adjust, being disappointed-- not wanted in Poland, not wanted here in the United States by my so-called family. But I was-- just-- I regained confidence-- faith in people and family on my mother's side. The family embraced me so nicely in Israel, and as a result, I found second cousins in the United States, with whom I'm corresponding over the telephone continuously.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has been your occupation here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to learn a vocation, many trades, and I stuck with the one vocation of being a butcher at meat markets. And I was very, very successful at one time, and unfortunately, it didn't work out for personal reasons. And I picked up the pieces, [INAUDIBLE] because the wonderful wife, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] Helen, she rest in peace, who died two years ago. She urged me to continue life and marry again and continue. For that reason, I'm keeping this house, and I want to marry again. That beautiful lady told me-- I'd like you to meet her. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you had two daughters. Did you raise them with Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: My late wife, Helen, raised them. I didn't know right from wrong. I didn't raise myself. I was a kid off the trains. I never had an opportunity to raise them. I'm self-educated because of Helen's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] help to me. She brought out the best of me, which I didn't think I have. As a result, I have a big library, and I'm very well read, and I lecture to different groups about my experiences. But I lecture also-- I was president of Evanston B'nai B'rith, one of-- a very prominent B'nai B'rith lodge. I've been vice president for Federation of Jewish Men's Club. It's one of my many worthy causes. Helen showed an example for me and my children how to belong and give of yourself to help others, because that's how I was helped to come to the United States, through an organization. And Helen raised my children. She raised me too, because I had an unfortunate marriage before Helen. Helen raised-- brought out the best in me and my children. As a result of Helen, my children are what they are today, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to end with a few personal questions. What kind of father were you to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- as far as I was concerned, I was trying my best not to have-- to give the things I didn't have. As you remember me telling you, that I said I don't want my children to grow up go-- need-- in need of anything which I didn't have, which means to make-- give them a nice place to live, give them the right opportunity to better their lots. They should have a nice place to live and go to a nice school. And Helen helped me. Helen gave up her profession to raise my children. She went to child guidance with them. She raised them the best, and nobody knew there was such a thing as parents' night. Helen was never married before. She never had no children. But somehow she was willing to learn, and she learned, and she showed the children a way. Right now, thank God, Eileen, she's a speech pathologist. Her husband's a CPA, is a partner-- a junior partner in an investment company. Richard, my son-in-law-- wonderful son-in-law-- have the best Jewish boys. And he is a supervisor for Kraft Foods, a program-- a computer programmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to make this tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to leave a legacy for others to see, especially my children and my grandchildren, to know that it could happen, what happened to me. Because you are so-- in the United States-- I don't mean to criticize. You are very charitable people. You are very generous. Every organization is manned by Jewish people, not only for Jewish causes, but for human causes. And-- but you are unaware of what will happen, what happened in Poland. In Spain, 500 years ago, before the Inquisition, Jews were [INAUDIBLE] the country of Spain, then expelled to Portugal, as we know the Inquisition. Germany-- German Jews were Germans before anything else, [INAUDIBLE]. The East European Jew was a poor Jew [INAUDIBLE], so him, it doesn't matter what happened. Even in Poland, we had very prosperous Jews. Forgot themselves as being Jewish and spoke Polish. They were assimilated Jews, but they had-- a lot befell on them too. What--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt it was important to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Important to us to see that it could happen again. For me to make the tape-- hopefully somebody will see it. And I didn't mean to insult any nationalities. I just told you what I have seen. I was a witness to it, and it's my duty to share whoever's watching it, or whoever will see it ever, to what I have seen and were a witness to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know that your children and your grandchildren, they will be watching this tape. Do you have one final message to say to your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children and my grandchildren, I was-- I thought I was a very wonderful father. I tried my best. I didn't-- I wasn't a womanizer. I didn't drink or smoke. I worked seven days a week, from early morning to late at night. Helen forced me to close one day my meat market. I worked by myself under very adverse conditions. Helen and I were mugged and beaten up and robbed twice in my meat market, and I continued doing it over and over again. I'm very proud that the opportunity was given to my daughters to marry nice, Jewish boys because of Helen's teaching them to go to Hebrew schools, in a Talmud synagogue Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They graduated from Hebrew school. They had-- their teeth were fixed. They had-- what do you call it? On the teeth. Braces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Braces. We couldn't afford food on the table, but my kids had braces. We couldn't afford food on the table, buy clothes for my [INAUDIBLE]. We  never bought no clothes, that which you call it necessities. We didn't have no necessities. We drive an old, beat-up jalopy. But my children lived in a nice home, had a proper opportunity. Yes, they were deprived. Helen was a disciplinarian. She grounded Eileen many a times. But look what came out of it. What she is now, the final product.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're proud of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very proud of my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you love them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love them to no end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Jerry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother, father, and my little brother, Ruven. My ho-- my father is holding him in his lap. And my little brother, Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abraham and my fathers-- you see, he look so old? So does my mother. It's because he was carrying a can of canister soda for the circumcision for the bris of my little brother, Ruven. A bunch of Poles beat him up and left him for dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The greenery you see in the background-- we lived near an ocean-- lake, and they sent-- were regularly sent-- my mother cultivated it with human waste, and we dug it up and we had flowers and all kinds of vegetables-- fruits and vegetables. As a result, I have a duplicate in my home yard-- in my home backyard here. This is a picture of my aunt, my mother's younger sister, and myself, in my hometown where I was born. And my aunt went off to Palestine, marrying a man for money, so that she could immigrate to Palestine. This is the aunt who I, unfortunately, didn't get in touch with after the war, when I found my family in Israel now. But I met her sis-- her children and her husband. And beautiful memories just looking at her picture and myself as I was a little child, here in the picture. This is a picture of my mother and my father standing in the background, in our back yard, with the vegetation in the back as you see it. In the beginning, to my right is my boyfriend and neighbor, Sam, and next to him, to my right, is myself. And next to myself in the white clothes is my brother, Abraham. And next is Ruven. And the end, over on the left, is-- is Menachem Boorstein, the brother of the extreme right. He is the only one, and myself, who have survived out of 25,000 Jewish people in the ghetto. Only two children have survived. This is a picture of myself in the war, on the train, selling candy charms to the black marketeers. And to accumulate enough money to buy the candy charms, I was singing songs. Composed my own song, and collected enough money to buy one kilogram of candy charms. This picture was given to my aunt in Palestine, and when I found my family in Israel, this picture was given to me. As you notice, I'm bare-footed, and I-- it took me a long while to accumulate enough money to buy myself my first pair of shoes. And the shoes-- the only money I have accumulated is to-- which I could afford to buy were too small for me. I squashed my feet into the shoes. The storekeeper didn't want to sell them to because they were too small for me, and I had frostbite as a result. I still have marks on my feet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself during the-- after the war in a kibbutz, Greifenberg, near Landsberg, the city where Hitler wrote As to my right, above my lapel, you see there's a little dog over there, the picture of a dog, because dogs somehow, during the war, even, warm-- kept me warm. Some [? familiarizing. ?] Horses and cows, I hugged to keep the warmth of the body, something to belong to me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself after I left the kibbutz Greifenberg, and I went to a displaced persons camp with Jews returning from Soviet Union and Neu Ulm. Ulm. U-L-M. And this is-- you see I held-- my right hand, I have a card. [INAUDIBLE] each time you go to the soup kitchen-- soup kitchen-- and you see a little container for the [INAUDIBLE]. I made a can with a wire to hold up the can. Every day, this is where we used to be-- got food to sustain ourselves in the displaced persons camp after the war, after I left the kibbutz on my way trying to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- this is a picture of Mr. And Mrs. Joseph Ciesinski. I ran away from the kibbutz in search of family in the United States on my stepfather's side, and I wrote to all the Ciesinskis if they're related to me. And Yiddish [INAUDIBLE]-- Yiddish names of my father, mother, my brothers, and myself, telling them I'm the only one who survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you related to me? Please answer me. This family, Joseph Ciesinski and his wife Mary, and his two daughters in the middle, have answered me and started writing to me and sending me packages, and they were going to bring me to the United States from--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I ran away from the kibbutz, so this way I wound up in Neu Ulm, and to be able to come to the United States. In the displaced persons camp in Neu Ulm, I met a family from my hometown who I didn't know before the war, and they urged me to write a Yiddish letter to my uncle so I would get more packages. And instead, after writing the Yiddish letter, all the letters stopped, and all the packages stopped coming. Bombarding this uncle of mine with letters in Polish and Yiddish, and explained to him that I'm sorry if I asked you for cigarettes and coffee. You asked me what you should send. Cigarettes and coffee I could sell. Now, to realize that this family, after I received a letter from a Mr. [? Nedboy, ?] a shoe manufacturing company owner, explaining to me in Yiddish that Mr. Ciesinski is my employee. I'm sorry to disappoint you that Mr. Ciesinski was a mix-up. He's not related to you. He's not even Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself when I have arrived in the United States of America, February 21st, 1948. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my late wife, Helen, and myself, who brought the best out in me. This is a picture of myself and my butcher's, who I just purchased from my boss, Wisconsin Packing Company and Meat Market. This was the grand opening, and I was about 30 years old, approximately. I'm right in the front in the middle. Zooming in right now, next to the-- sitting right in the front, next to the man with the glasses. Marsha-- my two daughters, Marsha and Eileen, the fruit of my labor and my effort. Marsha's on the right, and Eileen is on the left. This is a picture of my four grandchildren. In the beginning, on the right is Tara, and on the end is Michael. These are Marsha, the older one's, children. In the middle is Elena and Mark. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'd like you to meet part of my family. In the beginning, to my right, Marsha, my older daughter, Tara, her daughter. Michael, next to me. Behind him is Richard, my son-in-law-- Richard Weinstein. And next to me is my love, Toby, and I'm Jerry Shane And my daughter, Eileen and Danny and Elaina and Mark couldn't make it today, but they're still in my heart over here. Tara, I would like you to say something about [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: You tell me lots of stories, and I'm going to carry them on with me and tell my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Toby, would you like to say something about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would. I'd like to very much. I'd like to say, first of all, I'm very happy and proud to be part of the Shane family. They're-- they've made me very happy, the children, and especially Jerry. And I'm really very happy to be at this occasion. Jerry, you're a wonderful guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I love you, doll.
